Q1 — Place: The corner of my grandmother's living room had this old armchair upholstered in faded blue brocade with tiny gold flowers worn almost through to the canvas. The light from the south-facing window would fall on it in the afternoon and make the threads look like they were made of dust. I used to sit cross-legged on the floor beside it, leaning against its arm, pretending the chair was a person I was confiding in. The smell was specific — old library books and her lavender hand cream. I remember inventing names for the imaginary cat I was sure lived underneath it.
Q2 — Habit: I keep notebooks of overheard sentences. Not full conversations — single lines, scraps. Someone at a coffee shop saying 'I told him I wasn't his mother and he just looked at me like I'd grown a second head.' A woman on a train: 'the doctor said I had a poet's lung, whatever that means.' I have hundreds. I sometimes read them out loud just to hear how they sit in my mouth. My partner thinks it's a kind of theft, but I just like the way strangers' grammar reveals them. The wrong preposition, the unnecessary 'anyway.' It's like fingerprints.
Q3 — Explanation: I'll explain how a friendship ends. Not the dramatic kind — the slow drift. It works because at some point, one of you stops reaching for the same word for the same thing. You used to call something 'annoying' and you both knew the precise shade of annoying it was. Then one day you say 'annoying' and the other person hears it generically. The private dictionary you'd built starts losing entries. The friendship doesn't break — it gets unintelligible. Eventually you call each other less because the cost of translating yourself back to a stranger is too high.
Q4 — Conflict: My older brother and I had a vicious fight when I was 22 about whether our father had said 'I'm proud' or 'I'm proud of you' at my graduation. He insisted on the second. I insisted on the first. The argument lasted three hours. The surface story was about memory. The actual story was that the difference between those two sentences was enormous to me — the first was about him, the second was about me — and my brother couldn't see why I cared. I think what I really wanted him to say was that he understood why the missing two words mattered. He never did.
Q5 — Sound: The way my fourth-grade teacher said my name. She had a soft lisp on the s and she always paused for half a beat after the first syllable, like she was savoring it. No one has ever said my name that way before or since. I can hear it more clearly than her face. When I think of being looked-at by an adult who actually liked me, that's the sound — not a word but a specific way of giving someone their name back to them carefully.
Q6 — Body: I was in a job interview and the interviewer asked me a question about teamwork. Before I'd formed an answer, my throat had closed slightly and my hands had gone very still. My body had already decided something — I think it was: this woman is going to dislike me no matter what I say. And so my voice came out flatter than usual, and I gave a perfectly competent answer that landed nowhere. Walking home, I kept replaying the moment my body knew. The mind caught up about an hour later when I realized she'd been bored before I even started.
Q7 — Unworded: The exact, particular ache of finishing a book you didn't want to end. It's not just sadness about the characters being gone. It's a specific kind of mourning — for a self you only got to be while reading. The version of you that the book required. When the book ends, that version of you ends too. You go back to being the regular you, and the gap between them is the grief. No one I've described this to has ever quite known what I meant.
